# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between the Gauss Elimination method and the Gauss-Jordan Elimination method for solving a system of linear equations. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Gauss Elimination | Gauss-Jordan Elimination |
|---|---|---|
| Scope of row operations | Eliminates entries **only below** the pivot | Eliminates entries **both below and above** the pivot |
| Final form of system | Upper triangular matrix $U$ | Reduced row-echelon form $I$ (identity) |
| Solution retrieval | Requires **back substitution** | Read directly as the augmented column |
| Operation count | $\approx n^3 / 3$ | $\approx n^3 / 2$ (heavier) |

**Advantage of Gauss Elimination:** Lower computational cost; preferred when only forward elimination is needed.
**Advantage of Gauss-Jordan:** Useful for **computing the inverse** of a matrix $A$ by augmenting with $I$ and reducing to $[I \vert A^{-1}]$.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the condition of diagonal dominance. Why is it important in the context of the Gauss-Seidel iterative method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A matrix $A = [a_{ij}]$ of order $n$ is said to be **diagonally dominant** if for every row $i$:

$$\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \ne i}}^{n} \vert a_{ij} \vert$$

**Importance in Gauss-Seidel:** Diagonal dominance is a **sufficient condition for the convergence** of the Gauss-Seidel iteration. When the coefficient matrix satisfies this property, the spectral radius $\rho(B_{GS})$ of the iteration matrix lies strictly inside the unit disk, ensuring that successive iterates $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ contract toward the unique solution $\mathbf{x}^* = A^{-1} \mathbf{b}$ regardless of the initial guess.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark questions):** Students often write the *strict* inequality sign and forget to mention that the condition is **sufficient but not necessary**. Gauss-Seidel may still converge on certain non-diagonally-dominant matrices (e.g., symmetric positive definite ones), but you are *not expected* to derive this. Two marks are usually awarded for the definition and one mark for the convergence link.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Solve the following system of equations by the **Gauss Elimination method** (with partial pivoting if needed):

$$2x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 4$$
$$x_1 + 3x_2 + 2x_3 = 6$$
$$x_1 + x_2 + 3x_3 = 5$$

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Augmented matrix:**

$$[A \vert \mathbf{b}] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 & \vert & 4 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 & \vert & 6 \\ 1 & 1 & 3 & \vert & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Partial pivoting in column 1:** The largest absolute value in column 1 is $2$ (already in row 1), so no swap is needed.

**Step 3 — Eliminate $x_1$ from rows 2 and 3.** Multipliers:

$$m_{21} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5, \qquad m_{31} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

$$R_2 \to R_2 - 0.5 R_1: \quad [1 - 0.5(2),\ 3 - 0.5(1),\ 2 - 0.5(1) \vert 6 - 0.5(4)] = [0,\ 2.5,\ 1.5 \vert 4]$$

$$R_3 \to R_3 - 0.5 R_1: \quad [1 - 0.5(2),\ 1 - 0.5(1),\ 3 - 0.5(1) \vert 5 - 0.5(4)] = [0,\ 0.5,\ 2.5 \vert 3]$$

Updated matrix:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 & \vert & 4 \\ 0 & 2.5 & 1.5 & \vert & 4 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 2.5 & \vert & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $x_2$ from row 3.** Multiplier:

$$m_{32} = \frac{0.5}{2.5} = 0.2$$

$$R_3 \to R_3 - 0.2 R_2: \quad [0,\ 0.5 - 0.2(2.5),\ 2.5 - 0.2(1.5) \vert 3 - 0.2(4)] = [0,\ 0,\ 2.2 \vert 2.2]$$

Final upper-triangular form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 & \vert & 4 \\ 0 & 2.5 & 1.5 & \vert & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 2.2 & \vert & 2.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Back substitution:**

$$2.2 x_3 = 2.2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_3 = 1$$

$$2.5 x_2 + 1.5(1) = 4 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2.5 x_2 = 2.5 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_2 = 1$$

$$2 x_1 + 1 + 1 = 4 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2 x_1 = 2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_1 = 1$$

$$\boxed{\;x_1 = 1,\ x_2 = 1,\ x_3 = 1\;}$$

---

**(b)** Perform **two iterations** of the **Gauss-Seidel method** to solve:

$$8x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 10$$
$$x_1 + 7x_2 + x_3 = 9$$
$$x_1 + x_2 + 6x_3 = 8$$

Take the initial approximation as $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = (0, 0, 0)^T$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Check diagonal dominance:**

Row 1: $\vert 8 \vert > \vert 1 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 2$ ✓
Row 2: $\vert 7 \vert > \vert 1 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 2$ ✓
Row 3: $\vert 6 \vert > \vert 1 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 2$ ✓

The system is diagonally dominant, hence Gauss-Seidel will converge.

**Iteration formulae:**

$$x_1^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{8}\left(10 - x_2^{(k)} - x_3^{(k)}\right)$$

$$x_2^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{7}\left(9 - x_1^{(k+1)} - x_3^{(k)}\right)$$

$$x_3^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{6}\left(8 - x_1^{(k+1)} - x_2^{(k+1)}\right)$$

**Iteration 1** (using $x_2^{(0)} = x_3^{(0)} = 0$):

$$x_1^{(1)} = \frac{10 - 0 - 0}{8} = 1.250000$$

$$x_2^{(1)} = \frac{9 - 1.250000 - 0}{7} = \frac{7.750000}{7} = 1.107143$$

$$x_3^{(1)} = \frac{8 - 1.250000 - 1.107143}{6} = \frac{5.642857}{6} = 0.940476$$

**Iteration 2** (using $x_2^{(1)} = 1.107143$ and $x_3^{(1)} = 0.940476$):

$$x_1^{(2)} = \frac{10 - 1.107143 - 0.940476}{8} = \frac{7.952381}{8} = 0.994048$$

$$x_2^{(2)} = \frac{9 - 0.994048 - 0.940476}{7} = \frac{7.065476}{7} = 1.009354$$

$$x_3^{(2)} = \frac{8 - 0.994048 - 1.009354}{6} = \frac{5.996598}{6} = 0.999433$$

**Summary:**

| Iteration $k$ | $x_1^{(k)}$ | $x_2^{(k)}$ | $x_3^{(k)}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000000 | 0.000000 | 0.000000 |
| 1 | 1.250000 | 1.107143 | 0.940476 |
| 2 | 0.994048 | 1.009354 | 0.999433 |

After two iterations:

$$\boxed{\;x_1 \approx 0.9940,\quad x_2 \approx 1.0094,\quad x_3 \approx 0.9994\;}$$

The true solution is $\mathbf{x} = (1, 1, 1)^T$, and the iteration is clearly converging.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Solve the following system using the **Gauss-Jordan Elimination method**:

$$2x_1 + 3x_2 + x_3 = 9$$
$$x_1 + 2x_2 + 3x_3 = 6$$
$$3x_1 + x_2 + 2x_3 = 8$$

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Augmented matrix:**

$$[A \vert \mathbf{b}] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 1 & \vert & 9 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & \vert & 6 \\ 3 & 1 & 2 & \vert & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Partial pivoting in column 1:** Largest entry is $3$ in row 3, so swap $R_1 \leftrightarrow R_3$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 & 2 & \vert & 8 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & \vert & 6 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 & \vert & 9 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Scale row 1 to make the pivot unity:** $R_1 \to R_1 / 3$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1/3 & 2/3 & \vert & 8/3 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & \vert & 6 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 & \vert & 9 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $x_1$ from rows 2 and 3:**

$$R_2 \to R_2 - 1 \cdot R_1: \quad [0,\ 2 - 1/3,\ 3 - 2/3 \vert 6 - 8/3] = [0,\ 5/3,\ 7/3 \vert 10/3]$$

$$R_3 \to R_3 - 2 \cdot R_1: \quad [0,\ 3 - 2/3,\ 1 - 4/3 \vert 9 - 16/3] = [0,\ 7/3,\ -1/3 \vert 11/3]$$

**Step 5 — Scale row 2 to make pivot unity:** $R_2 \to R_2 \cdot (3/5)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1/3 & 2/3 & \vert & 8/3 \\ 0 & 1 & 7/5 & \vert & 2 \\ 0 & 7/3 & -1/3 & \vert & 11/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Eliminate $x_2$ from rows 1 and 3:**

$$R_1 \to R_1 - (1/3) R_2: \quad [1,\ 0,\ 2/3 - 7/15 \vert 8/3 - 2/3] = [1,\ 0,\ 1/5 \vert 2]$$

$$R_3 \to R_3 - (7/3) R_2: \quad [0,\ 0,\ -1/3 - 49/15 \vert 11/3 - 14/3] = [0,\ 0,\ -18/5 \vert -1]$$

**Step 7 — Scale row 3:** $R_3 \to R_3 \cdot (-5/18)$:

$$R_3 = [0,\ 0,\ 1 \vert 5/18]$$

**Step 8 — Back elimination of $x_3$ from row 1:**

$$R_1 \to R_1 - (1/5) R_3: \quad [1,\ 0,\ 0 \vert 2 - 1/18] = [1,\ 0,\ 0 \vert 35/18]$$

**Step 9 — Back elimination of $x_2$ and $x_3$ from row 2:**

$$R_2 \to R_2 - (7/5) R_3: \quad [0,\ 1,\ 0 \vert 2 - 7/18] = [0,\ 1,\ 0 \vert 29/18]$$

Final reduced row-echelon form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & \vert & 35/18 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \vert & 29/18 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \vert & 5/18 \end{bmatrix}$$

Wait — let us verify by substitution. Plugging into the original equations:
- Eq. 1: $2(35/18) + 3(29/18) + 1(5/18) = (70 + 87 + 5)/18 = 162/18 = 9$ ✓
- Eq. 2: $(35/18) + 2(29/18) + 3(5/18) = (35 + 58 + 15)/18 = 108/18 = 6$ ✓
- Eq. 3: $3(35/18) + (29/18) + 2(5/18) = (105 + 29 + 10)/18 = 144/18 = 8$ ✓

$$\boxed{\;x_1 = 35/18 \approx 1.9444,\quad x_2 = 29/18 \approx 1.6111,\quad x_3 = 5/18 \approx 0.2778\;}$$

---

**(b)** Using the **LU Decomposition (Doolittle's scheme)**, factorise:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

and hence solve $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ where $\mathbf{b} = (8, 9, 7)^T$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construct $L$ and $U$:**

$$L = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ \ell_{21} & 1 & 0 \\ \ell_{31} & \ell_{32} & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad U = \begin{bmatrix} u_{11} & u_{12} & u_{13} \\ 0 & u_{22} & u_{23} \\ 0 & 0 & u_{33} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Row 1 of $U$:**

$$u_{11} = 4,\quad u_{12} = 1,\quad u_{13} = 2$$

**Column 1 of $L$:**

$$\ell_{21} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5, \qquad \ell_{31} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Row 2 of $U$:**

$$u_{22} = 5 - (0.5)(1) = 4.5$$

$$u_{23} = 1 - (0.5)(2) = 0$$

**Column 2 of $L$:**

$$\ell_{32} = \frac{2 - (0.25)(1)}{4.5} = \frac{1.75}{4.5} = 0.388889$$

**Row 3 of $U$:**

$$u_{33} = 4 - (0.25)(2) - (0.388889)(0) = 4 - 0.5 - 0 = 3.5$$

Therefore:

$$L = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 1 & 0 \\ 0.25 & 0.388889 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad U = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 4.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Forward substitution** $L\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$y_1 = 8$$

$$0.5(8) + y_2 = 9 \quad\Rightarrow\quad y_2 = 9 - 4 = 5$$

$$0.25(8) + 0.388889(5) + y_3 = 7 \quad\Rightarrow\quad y_3 = 7 - 2 - 1.944444 = 3.055556$$

**Step 3 — Back substitution** $U\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$:

$$3.5 x_3 = 3.055556 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_3 = 0.873016$$

$$4.5 x_2 + 0(0.873016) = 5 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_2 = 1.111111$$

$$4 x_1 + 1(1.111111) + 2(0.873016) = 8 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 4 x_1 = 8 - 1.111111 - 1.746032 = 5.142857 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_1 = 1.285714$$

**Verification:**
- $4(1.285714) + 1(1.111111) + 2(0.873016) = 5.142857 + 1.111111 + 1.746032 = 8.000$ ✓
- $2(1.285714) + 5(1.111111) + 1(0.873016) = 2.571429 + 5.555556 + 0.873016 = 9.000$ ✓
- $1(1.285714) + 2(1.111111) + 4(0.873016) = 1.285714 + 2.222222 + 3.492063 = 7.000$ ✓

$$\boxed{\;L = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 1 & 0 \\ 0.25 & 0.388889 & 1 \end{bmatrix},\quad U = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 4.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3.5 \end{bmatrix},\quad \mathbf{x} = (1.2857,\ 1.1111,\ 0.8730)^T\;}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Part B 14-mark questions):**
> 1. **Arithmetic slip in multiplier** (loss of 2 marks): Always re-check $m_{ik} = a_{ik} / a_{kk}$ by mental calculation. A single wrong multiplier cascades into all subsequent rows.
> 2. **Skipping back substitution step-by-step** (loss of 2 marks): Examiners want to see each row's equation written explicitly *and* the algebraic isolation of each variable. Don't jump straight to the answer.
> 3. **Forgetting partial pivoting in Gauss-Jordan / LU** (loss of 1–2 marks): If a pivot is zero or near-zero, you must state "no pivot is needed since the largest absolute value is already in the diagonal position" or perform the swap explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing Jacobi with Gauss-Seidel** (loss of 3 marks): The defining feature of Gauss-Seidel is the use of the *just-computed* value within the same iteration. If you write $x_2^{(k+1)}$ using $x_1^{(k)}$, you have written Jacobi, not Gauss-Seidel. **Always use $x_1^{(k+1)}$ inside the same iteration.**
> 5. **LU decomposition — wrong sign convention** (loss of 1 mark): In Doolittle, $l_{ii} = 1$ (unit lower triangular). In Crout, $u_{ii} = 1$ (unit upper triangular). Do not mix them up.
> 6. **Not verifying diagonal dominance for Gauss-Seidel** (loss of 1 mark): Always show the inequality explicitly *before* starting iterations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gauss Elimination** uses forward elimination followed by back substitution; its complexity is $O(n^3/3)$.
- **Gauss-Jordan Elimination** is Gauss elimination with extra back-elimination; useful for **matrix inversion** and **simplex tableau reduction** in LP.
- **Partial pivoting** swaps rows to put the largest-magnitude element on the pivot position, controlling round-off error growth.
- **LU Decomposition** rewrites $A = LU$ and reduces the cost of solving $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ for **multiple RHS vectors** to just two triangular solves per $\mathbf{b}$.
- **Doolittle** has $l_{ii} = 1$; **Crout** has $u_{ii} = 1$; **Cholesky** ($A = LL^T$) is restricted to **symmetric positive definite** matrices.
- **Gauss-Seidel** is an **iterative** method that updates components *in place* using the most recent values, giving roughly **twice the convergence speed** of the Jacobi method for well-conditioned systems.
- **Diagonal dominance** ($\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{j \ne i} \vert a_{ij} \vert$) is a **sufficient (not necessary)** condition for Gauss-Seidel convergence.
- The **stopping criterion** for Gauss-Seidel is $\max_i \vert x_i^{(k+1)} - x_i^{(k)} \vert < \varepsilon$, typically with $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$ or $10^{-5}$.
- In **engineering practice**, Gauss/LU are used for small dense matrices; Gauss-Seidel (or its accelerated variants like SOR) is used for large sparse systems arising in PDE solvers and circuit simulation.
- **Operation count ranking (best to worst)**: Gauss-Seidel per iteration ($2n^2$) < Gauss ($n^3/3$) < Gauss-Jordan ($n^3/2$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curve Fitting: Least Square Regression - Linear Regression, Linearization of Nonlinear Relationships, Polynomial and Multiple Linear Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET833 - NUMERICAL TECHNIQUES  ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Curve Fitting: Least Square Regression - Linear Regression, Linearization of Nonlinear Relationships, Polynomial and Multiple Linear Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Curve Fitting** is the process of constructing a mathematical function (or curve) that best fits a series of data points, typically derived from experimental observations or empirical measurements. In the context of the **Least Squares Method**, the objective is to minimize the sum of the squares of the vertical deviations (residuals) between the observed data points and the values predicted by the fitted curve.

Mathematically, for a given set of $n$ data points $(x_i, y_i)$ and a candidate function $f(x)$, the least-squares criterion seeks parameters that minimize:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i - f(x_i) \right]^2$$

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the syllabus explicitly requires mastery of:
1. **Linear Regression** — fitting a straight line $y = a + bx$.
2. **Linearization of Nonlinear Relationships** — converting exponential, power, and reciprocal models into a linear form.
3. **Polynomial Regression** — fitting $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_m x^m$.
4. **Multiple Linear Regression** — fitting $y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 + \cdots$ with more than one independent variable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight):**
> The **Method of Least Squares** is a statistical optimization technique that determines the parameters of a regression model by minimizing the objective function $S = \sum (y_i - f(x_i))^2$. The fitted curve is called the **regression curve**, and the process is known as **regression analysis**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Best Fit Thread" Model

Imagine you have scattered iron filings on a sheet of paper, and you want to thread a string through them so it represents the "trend" of the cloud.

- If the filings are arranged roughly along a **straight path**, the string will be a **straight line** (Linear Regression).
- If the filings curve like the path of a thrown ball, the string will need to **bend** — you would use a **polynomial** curve (Polynomial Regression).
- If the cloud's shape is curved (e.g., exponential growth of bacteria), the string itself may be straight, but the **graph paper must be logarithmic** to "straighten" the data (Linearization).
- If the cloud depends on **two inputs** (e.g., temperature AND pressure affecting yield), the string becomes a **plane in 3D space** (Multiple Linear Regression).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Critical Note:**
> KTU examiners test *both* the theoretical derivation of normal equations *and* the numerical computation of regression coefficients. Always carry out the full $\Sigma$ table before substituting into the normal equations.

### 1.3 Why Curve Fitting Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Domain | Application of Curve Fitting |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Stress–strain curves, settlement vs. load |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Engine performance maps, heat transfer data |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Signal noise filtering, sensor calibration |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Reaction rate vs. concentration studies |
| **Biomedical Engineering** | Pharmacokinetic dose-response modelling |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Philosophy of "Why Least Squares?"

The principle of least squares was independently developed by **Carl Friedrich Gauss** and **Adrien-Marie Legendre** around 1800. The deep reasoning is rooted in probability theory (Gauss–Markov theorem): when measurement errors follow a **normal (Gaussian) distribution**, the least-squares estimator is the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** — i.e., it has the minimum variance among all unbiased linear estimators.

**Intuitive Justification:**
- *Why squares?* Because squaring penalizes large errors much more heavily than small ones. A single bad data point should "pull" the curve toward it, but not dominate.
- *Why not absolute values?* Because $|y - f(x)|$ is not differentiable at the minimum (corner), making it mathematically difficult to optimize analytically.

### 2.2 Linear Regression — $y = a + bx$

**Setup:** Given $n$ data points $(x_i, y_i)$, fit the straight line $y = a + bx$ that minimizes:

$$S(a, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$$

**Derivation Logic:**
1. Take partial derivatives of $S$ with respect to $a$ and $b$ and set them to zero.
2. This yields the **Normal Equations**.
3. Solve the resulting $2 \times 2$ linear system.

### 2.3 Linearization of Nonlinear Models

Nonlinear models can be transformed into a linear form through **variable substitutions**, after which the standard linear regression applies.

### 2.4 Polynomial Regression — $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_m x^m$

The problem reduces to a **linear system of $m+1$ normal equations** in $m+1$ unknowns. Although the model is "polynomial in $x$", it is **linear in the parameters** $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_m$ — hence least squares applies directly.

### 2.5 Multiple Linear Regression — $y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 + \cdots + b_k x_k$

With $k$ independent variables, we obtain $k+1$ normal equations in $k+1$ unknowns. For $k=2$, the model is $y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2$, which geometrically represents a **plane in 3D space**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference (Mandatory for KTU Board Exam)**

| # | Model Type | Original Form | Linearized Form | Slope Analogue | Intercept Analogue |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear** | $y = a + bx$ | $Y = A + BX$ | $b = \dfrac{n\Sigma xy - \Sigma x \Sigma y}{n\Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2}$ | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ |
| 2 | **Exponential** | $y = a e^{bx}$ | $\ln y = \ln a + bx$ | $b = \dfrac{n\Sigma x \ln y - \Sigma x \Sigma \ln y}{n\Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2}$ | $a = e^{\bar{\ln y} - b\bar{x}}$ |
| 3 | **Power** | $y = a x^{b}$ | $\log y = \log a + b \log x$ | $b = \dfrac{n\Sigma \log x \log y - \Sigma \log x \Sigma \log y}{n\Sigma (\log x)^2 - (\Sigma \log x)^2}$ | $a = 10^{(\bar{\log y} - b\bar{\log x})}$ |
| 4 | **Reciprocal** | $y = a + \dfrac{b}{x}$ | $y = a + b \cdot u$ where $u = \dfrac{1}{x}$ | $b = \dfrac{n\Sigma uy - \Sigma u \Sigma y}{n\Sigma u^2 - (\Sigma u)^2}$ | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{u}$ |
| 5 | **Growth** | $y = a b^{x}$ | $\log y = \log a + x \log b$ | $\log b = \dfrac{n\Sigma x \log y - \Sigma x \Sigma \log y}{n\Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2}$ | $\log a = \bar{\log y} - (\log b)\bar{x}$ |
| 6 | **Polynomial (degree 2)** | $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$ | Solve $3 \times 3$ normal system (see §3) | — | — |
| 7 | **Multiple (2 variables)** | $y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2$ | Solve $3 \times 3$ normal system (see §3) | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Denominator Form:** Many KTU students lose marks by writing the denominator as $n\Sigma x^2 - \Sigma x^2$ instead of the correct $n\Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2$. The squared term refers to the *square of the sum*, NOT the sum of squares of $x$.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Quality Control:** Linear regression on calibration data validates sensor accuracy.
- **Predictive Analytics:** Power-law fits describe fractal dimensions, allometric scaling in biology.
- **Process Engineering:** Polynomial regression captures nonlinearities in chemical reactors.
- **Econometrics:** Multiple regression models GDP as a function of inflation, unemployment, etc.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Normal Equations for Linear Regression $y = a + bx$

**Objective:** Minimize $S(a, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$.

**Step 1 — Expand the objective function:**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i^2 + a^2 + b^2 x_i^2 - 2 a y_i - 2 b x_i y_i + 2 ab x_i \right)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $a$ and set to zero (first-order necessary condition):**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( 2a - 2y_i + 2b x_i \right) = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = n a + b \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $b$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( 2 b x_i^2 - 2 x_i y_i + 2 a x_i \right) = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i = a \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + b \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2$$

**Step 4 — Compact Form of the Normal Equations:**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \Sigma y &= n a + b \Sigma x \\ \Sigma xy &= a \Sigma x + b \Sigma x^2 \end{aligned}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $b$ (Cramer's Rule applied to the $2 \times 2$ system):**

$$b = \frac{n \Sigma xy - \Sigma x \Sigma y}{n \Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $a$ using the mean values:**

$$a = \bar{y} - b \bar{x} = \frac{\Sigma y}{n} - b \cdot \frac{\Sigma x}{n}$$

### 3.2 Derivation for Polynomial Regression (Degree 2) — $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$

**Objective:** Minimize $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i - a_2 x_i^2)^2$.

**Step 1 — Set partial derivatives to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_0} = -2 \sum (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i - a_2 x_i^2) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_1} = -2 \sum x_i (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i - a_2 x_i^2) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_2} = -2 \sum x_i^2 (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i - a_2 x_i^2) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to obtain the normal equations:**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \Sigma y &= n a_0 + a_1 \Sigma x + a_2 \Sigma x^2 \\ \Sigma xy &= a_0 \Sigma x + a_1 \Sigma x^2 + a_2 \Sigma x^3 \\ \Sigma x^2 y &= a_0 \Sigma x^2 + a_1 \Sigma x^3 + a_2 \Sigma x^4 \end{aligned}}$$

**Step 3 — Matrix Representation:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} n & \Sigma x & \Sigma x^2 \\ \Sigma x & \Sigma x^2 & \Sigma x^3 \\ \Sigma x^2 & \Sigma x^3 & \Sigma x^4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \Sigma y \\ \Sigma xy \\ \Sigma x^2 y \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This symmetric matrix is the **Gram matrix** of the basis $\{1, x, x^2\}$. It is positive definite for distinct $x_i$, guaranteeing a unique solution.

### 3.3 Derivation for Multiple Linear Regression (2 variables) — $y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2$

**Objective:** Minimize $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - b_0 - b_1 x_{1i} - b_2 x_{2i})^2$.

**Step 1 — Set partial derivatives to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b_0} = -2 \sum (y_i - b_0 - b_1 x_{1i} - b_2 x_{2i}) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b_1} = -2 \sum x_{1i}(y_i - b_0 - b_1 x_{1i} - b_2 x_{2i}) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b_2} = -2 \sum x_{2i}(y_i - b_0 - b_1 x_{1i} - b_2 x_{2i}) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to obtain the normal equations:**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \Sigma y &= n b_0 + b_1 \Sigma x_1 + b_2 \Sigma x_2 \\ \Sigma x_1 y &= b_0 \Sigma x_1 + b_1 \Sigma x_1^2 + b_2 \Sigma x_1 x_2 \\ \Sigma x_2 y &= b_0 \Sigma x_2 + b_1 \Sigma x_1 x_2 + b_2 \Sigma x_2^2 \end{aligned}}$$

**Step 3 — Matrix Representation:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} n & \Sigma x_1 & \Sigma x_2 \\ \Sigma x_1 & \Sigma x_1^2 & \Sigma x_1 x_2 \\ \Sigma x_2 & \Sigma x_1 x_2 & \Sigma x_2^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} b_0 \\ b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \Sigma y \\ \Sigma x_1 y \\ \Sigma x_2 y \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Linear Regression (End-to-End)

**Problem:** Fit a straight line $y = a + bx$ to the data using least squares.

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 2 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 |

**Step 1 — Construct the $\Sigma$ table:**

| $x$ | $y$ | $xy$ | $x^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 2 | 1 |
| 2 | 4 | 8 | 4 |
| 3 | 5 | 15 | 9 |
| 4 | 4 | 16 | 16 |
| 5 | 5 | 25 | 25 |
| **$\Sigma$ = 15** | **$\Sigma$ = 20** | **$\Sigma$ = 66** | **$\Sigma$ = 55** |

So $n=5$, $\Sigma x = 15$, $\Sigma y = 20$, $\Sigma xy = 66$, $\Sigma x^2 = 55$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the formula for $b$:**

$$b = \frac{n \Sigma xy - \Sigma x \Sigma y}{n \Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2} = \frac{5 \times 66 - 15 \times 20}{5 \times 55 - (15)^2}$$

$$b = \frac{330 - 300}{275 - 225} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.6$$

**Step 3 — Compute $a$:**

$$a = \frac{\Sigma y - b \Sigma x}{n} = \frac{20 - 0.6 \times 15}{5} = \frac{20 - 9}{5} = \frac{11}{5} = 2.2$$

**Step 4 — Final fitted line:**

$$\boxed{y = 2.2 + 0.6 x}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Linearization (Exponential Fit)

**Problem:** Fit $y = a e^{bx}$ to the data.

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 1.2 | 1.8 | 2.7 | 4.0 | 6.1 |

**Step 1 — Linearize:** Take natural log of both sides: $\ln y = \ln a + bx$.

**Step 2 — Construct table with $X = x$, $Y = \ln y$:**

| $x$ | $\ln y$ | $x \ln y$ | $x^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.1823 | 0.1823 | 1 |
| 2 | 0.5878 | 1.1756 | 4 |
| 3 | 0.9933 | 2.9799 | 9 |
| 4 | 1.3863 | 5.5452 | 16 |
| 5 | 1.8083 | 9.0415 | 25 |
| **$\Sigma$ = 15** | **$\Sigma$ = 4.958** | **$\Sigma$ = 18.9245** | **$\Sigma$ = 55** |

**Step 3 — Compute slope $b$ (treating $\ln a$ as the intercept analogue):**

$$b = \frac{5 \times 18.9245 - 15 \times 4.958}{5 \times 55 - 225} = \frac{94.6225 - 74.37}{275 - 225} = \frac{20.2525}{50} = 0.40505$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\ln a$:**

$$\ln a = \frac{\Sigma \ln y - b \Sigma x}{n} = \frac{4.958 - 0.40505 \times 15}{5} = \frac{4.958 - 6.0758}{5} = -0.2236$$

**Step 5 — Recover $a$:**

$$a = e^{-0.2236} \approx 0.7996$$

**Step 6 — Final model:**

$$\boxed{y \approx 0.8 \, e^{0.405 x}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Master Workflow — The Least-Squares Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START - Collect Data Points xi, yi] --> B[Choose Candidate Model Type]
    B --> C{Is the Model Linear in Parameters?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Formulate Normal Equations]
    C -- No --> E[Apply Linearization Substitution]
    E --> F[Transform to Linear Form Y = A + BX]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Construct Sigma Table of Required Sums]
    G --> H[Solve the Linear System using Cramer or Matrix Inversion]
    H --> I[Compute Coefficients a and b]
    I --> J[Write Fitted Equation y = f of x]
    J --> K[Validate - Compute Sum of Squared Residuals S]
    K --> L{S is Acceptable?}
    L -- Yes --> M[END - Final Regression Model Ready]
    L -- No --> N[Revise Model Type and Restart from B]
```

### 4.2 Linearization Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Given Nonlinear Data] --> Q{Examine Shape of Scatter Plot}
    Q -- Curved, rising rapidly --> R{Does y grow by a constant factor per unit x?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Use Exponential Model y = a e to power bx]
    R -- No --> T{Does log y versus log x look linear?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Use Power Model y = a x to power b]
    T -- No --> V{Does y approach a horizontal asymptote as x grows?}
    V -- Yes --> W[Use Reciprocal Model y = a + b slash x]
    V -- No --> X[Use Polynomial Regression of degree 2 or 3]
    S --> Y[Apply Linearization - Transform then Solve]
    U --> Y
    W --> Y
    X --> Y
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Multiple Linear Regression Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Input Dataset with k Independent Variables] --> MATRIX[Construct Design Matrix X of order n by k+1]
    MATRIX --> NORMAL[Form Normal Equation Matrix X transpose X]
    NORMAL --> INVERSE[Compute Inverse of X transpose X]
    INVERSE --> MULTIPLY[Multiply by X transpose Y]
    MULTIPLY --> COEFFS[Output Regression Coefficients b0 to bk]
    COEFFS --> MODEL[Final Model y equals b0 plus b1 x1 plus ... plus bk xk]
    COEFFS --> VALIDATE[Cross Validation - Compute R squared]
    VALIDATE --> DEPLOY[Engineering Deployment]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture of Regression Modules in KTU Syllabus

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MOD1[Module 1 - Curve Fitting Domain]
        A1[Linear Regression y = a + bx] --> CORE[Normal Equation Core Engine]
        A2[Polynomial Regression y = polynomial in x] --> CORE
        A3[Multiple Linear Regression y = sum of weighted variables] --> CORE
        A4[Linearization Engine] --> CORE
    end
    CORE --> OUTPUT1[Best Fit Parameters]
    CORE --> OUTPUT2[Residual Analysis S]
    CORE --> OUTPUT3[Predictive Equation]
    OUTPUT1 --> REPORT[Engineering Decision Report]
    OUTPUT2 --> REPORT
    OUTPUT3 --> REPORT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note (KTU 2024):** The "Normal Equation Core Engine" is the unifying mathematical backbone of all four regression variants. Students who internalize this engine can answer any KTU Module 1 sub-question on curve fitting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*State the principle of least squares. Derive the normal equations for fitting a straight line $y = a + bx$ to a set of $n$ data points.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Principle (1 Mark):** The principle of least squares states that the best-fit curve is the one for which the sum of the squares of the deviations (residuals) of the observed values from the predicted values is a minimum.

**Objective function (1 Mark):** For the model $y = a + bx$, define:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - b x_i)^2$$

**Normal Equations (1 Mark):** Setting $\partial S / \partial a = 0$ and $\partial S / \partial b = 0$ yields:

$$\Sigma y = n a + b \Sigma x, \quad \Sigma xy = a \Sigma x + b \Sigma x^2$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Explain how an exponential model $y = a e^{bx}$ can be fitted using linear regression. Mention the necessary transformation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** The model $y = a e^{bx}$ is nonlinear in parameters. To apply linear least squares, take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln y = \ln a + b x$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Substitute $Y = \ln y$, $A = \ln a$, $B = b$, $X = x$. The equation reduces to the linear form $Y = A + BX$.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Apply the standard linear regression formulas to $(X, Y) = (x, \ln y)$ data. After computing $B$ and $A$, recover $a = e^A$ and $b = B$. The fitted model is $y = e^A \cdot e^{Bx}$.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023 — Module 1 Choice Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Fit a straight line $y = a + bx$ to the following data using the method of least squares:*

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 1 | 1.8 | 3.3 | 4.7 | 6.0 |

*Hence estimate $y$ at $x = 5$.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Derive the normal equations for fitting the polynomial $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$ using the method of least squares. Write the resulting system in matrix form.*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Sigma Table (2 Marks):**

| $x$ | $y$ | $xy$ | $x^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.0 | 0.0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1.8 | 1.8 | 1 |
| 2 | 3.3 | 6.6 | 4 |
| 3 | 4.7 | 14.1 | 9 |
| 4 | 6.0 | 24.0 | 16 |
| **$\Sigma$ = 10** | **$\Sigma$ = 16.8** | **$\Sigma$ = 46.5** | **$\Sigma$ = 30** |

**Step 2 — Apply formulas (2 Marks):**

$$b = \frac{n \Sigma xy - \Sigma x \Sigma y}{n \Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2} = \frac{5 \times 46.5 - 10 \times 16.8}{5 \times 30 - (10)^2}$$

$$b = \frac{232.5 - 168}{150 - 100} = \frac{64.5}{50} = 1.29$$

$$a = \frac{\Sigma y - b \Sigma x}{n} = \frac{16.8 - 1.29 \times 10}{5} = \frac{16.8 - 12.9}{5} = \frac{3.9}{5} = 0.78$$

**Step 3 — Fitted line and prediction (3 Marks):**

$$\boxed{y = 0.78 + 1.29 x}$$

At $x = 5$:

$$y(5) = 0.78 + 1.29 \times 5 = 0.78 + 6.45 = 7.23$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Objective Function (2 Marks):** For $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$, minimize:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i - a_2 x_i^2)^2$$

**Step 2 — Partial Derivatives (2 Marks):** Setting $\partial S / \partial a_0 = 0$, $\partial S / \partial a_1 = 0$, $\partial S / \partial a_2 = 0$ yields:

**Step 3 — Normal Equations (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned} \Sigma y &= n a_0 + a_1 \Sigma x + a_2 \Sigma x^2 \\ \Sigma x y &= a_0 \Sigma x + a_1 \Sigma x^2 + a_2 \Sigma x^3 \\ \Sigma x^2 y &= a_0 \Sigma x^2 + a_1 \Sigma x^3 + a_2 \Sigma x^4 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Matrix Form (1 Mark):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} n & \Sigma x & \Sigma x^2 \\ \Sigma x & \Sigma x^2 & \Sigma x^3 \\ \Sigma x^2 & \Sigma x^3 & \Sigma x^4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \Sigma y \\ \Sigma x y \\ \Sigma x^2 y \end{bmatrix}$$

---

**QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2022 — Module 1 Choice Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *The data below is suspected to follow an exponential law $y = a b^x$. Using least squares, find the best-fit values of $a$ and $b$.*

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 1.0 | 2.5 | 6.0 | 15.0 | 37.0 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** *For a multiple linear regression model $y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2$, write down the normal equations. Mention the geometric interpretation of this model.*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Linearize (1 Mark):** $y = a b^x \Rightarrow \log_{10} y = \log_{10} a + x \log_{10} b$. Let $Y = \log y$, $A = \log a$, $B = \log b$.

**Step 2 — Transformed Sigma Table (2 Marks):**

| $x$ | $y$ | $Y = \log y$ | $xY$ | $x^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0 |
| 1 | 2.5 | 0.3979 | 0.3979 | 1 |
| 2 | 6.0 | 0.7782 | 1.5564 | 4 |
| 3 | 15.0 | 1.1761 | 3.5283 | 9 |
| 4 | 37.0 | 1.5682 | 6.2728 | 16 |
| **$\Sigma$ = 10** | | **$\Sigma Y$ = 3.9204** | **$\Sigma xY$ = 11.7554** | **$\Sigma x^2$ = 30** |

**Step 3 — Slope $B = \log b$ (2 Marks):**

$$B = \frac{n\Sigma xY - \Sigma x \Sigma Y}{n\Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2} = \frac{5 \times 11.7554 - 10 \times 3.9204}{5 \times 30 - 100} = \frac{58.777 - 39.204}{50} = \frac{19.573}{50} = 0.39146$$

**Step 4 — Intercept $A = \log a$ and recover $a, b$ (2 Marks):**

$$A = \frac{\Sigma Y - B \Sigma x}{n} = \frac{3.9204 - 0.39146 \times 10}{5} = \frac{3.9204 - 3.9146}{5} = \frac{0.0058}{5} \approx 0.00116$$

$$a = 10^{0.00116} \approx 1.0027, \quad b = 10^{0.39146} \approx 2.4645$$

$$\boxed{y \approx 1.003 \times (2.465)^x}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Normal Equations (4 Marks):** Minimizing $S = \Sigma (y_i - b_0 - b_1 x_{1i} - b_2 x_{2i})^2$ and setting partials to zero:

$$\begin{aligned} \Sigma y &= n b_0 + b_1 \Sigma x_1 + b_2 \Sigma x_2 \\ \Sigma x_1 y &= b_0 \Sigma x_1 + b_1 \Sigma x_1^2 + b_2 \Sigma x_1 x_2 \\ \Sigma x_2 y &= b_0 \Sigma x_2 + b_1 \Sigma x_1 x_2 + b_2 \Sigma x_2^2 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Geometric Interpretation (3 Marks):** The model $y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2$ represents a **plane in three-dimensional space** $(x_1, x_2, y)$. Here, $b_0$ is the intercept on the $y$-axis, $b_1$ is the partial slope of the plane in the $x_1$ direction, and $b_2$ is the partial slope in the $x_2$ direction. The least-squares fit minimizes the sum of squared vertical distances from the data points to this plane.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sigma Squared Confusion (–2 Marks):** Writing $n\Sigma x^2 - \Sigma x^2$ instead of $n\Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2$ — the denominator is the square of the *sum*, not the sum of squares.
> 2. **Linearization without Recovering Coefficients (–2 Marks):** Students often compute $B$ and $A$ but forget to back-transform to obtain the original $a$ and $b$ in nonlinear models.
> 3. **Misuse of Log vs Ln (–1 Mark):** KTU expects consistency. If you start with $\log_{10}$, use $\log_{10}$ throughout.
> 4. **Skipping the Sigma Table (–1 Mark):** Always show the $\Sigma$ table explicitly. Examiners award partial marks for this.
> 5. **Polynomial Matrix Errors (–2 Marks):** In polynomial regression, the off-diagonal entries in the Gram matrix must be symmetric (e.g., $\Sigma x^3$ appears in both positions $(1,3)$ and $(3,1)$).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Module 1 Revision Cram Sheet**

- ✅ **Least Squares Criterion:** Minimize $S = \Sigma (y_i - f(x_i))^2$.
- ✅ **Linear Regression Form:** $y = a + bx$ — always construct the **$\Sigma$ table** for $x, y, xy, x^2$.
- ✅ **Slope Formula:** $b = \dfrac{n\Sigma xy - \Sigma x \Sigma y}{n\Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2}$.
- ✅ **Intercept Formula:** $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = \dfrac{\Sigma y - b\Sigma x}{n}$.
- ✅ **Linearization Recipe:** Substitute $X, Y$ variables, solve linear system, then **back-transform** to recover original parameters.
- ✅ **Exponential Model:** $y = a e^{bx} \Rightarrow \ln y = \ln a + bx$. Recover $a = e^{\ln a}$.
- ✅ **Power Model:** $y = ax^b \Rightarrow \log y = \log a + b \log x$. Recover $a = 10^{\log a}$.
- ✅ **Growth Model:** $y = ab^x \Rightarrow \log y = \log a + x \log b$. Recover both $a$ and $b$ via $10^{(\cdot)}$.
- ✅ **Reciprocal Model:** $y = a + b/x \Rightarrow$ substitute $u = 1/x$, then use linear regression on $(u, y)$.
- ✅ **Polynomial Degree 2 System:** Three normal equations with symmetric $3 \times 3$ Gram matrix.
- ✅ **Multiple Regression (2 variables):** $y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2$ — also a $3 \times 3$ symmetric system.
- ✅ **Geometric Intuition:** Linear = line, Polynomial = curve, Multiple (2 var) = plane in 3D.
- ✅ **Gram Matrix:** For polynomial of degree $m$, the entry $(i,j)$ is $\Sigma x^{i+j}$ for $i,j = 0, 1, \ldots, m$.
- ✅ **Symmetry Rule:** The coefficient matrix is always symmetric — exploit this property in numerical solvers.
- ✅ **Gauss–Markov Connection:** Least squares is BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator) under Gaussian error assumption.
- ✅ **Examiner Trick:** They will give noisy exponential data and test whether you linearize correctly before fitting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interpolation - Newton's Forward, Backward, Divided Difference, Lagrange's Interpolation, Cubic Spline
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET833 - NUMERICAL TECHNIQUES ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interpolation - Newton's Forward, Backward, Divided Difference, Lagrange's Interpolation, Cubic Spline

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — INTERPOLATION TECHNIQUES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Interpolation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpolation** is the process of estimating an intermediate (unknown) value of a function $y = f(x)$ for a given value of $x$ that lies **between** known tabulated data points $(x_0, y_0), (x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$, by constructing a simpler function (typically a polynomial) that **passes exactly through all the given data points**.

The constructed polynomial is called the **interpolating polynomial**, denoted $P(x)$, and it must satisfy:

$$P(x_i) = f(x_i) = y_i, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

### 1.2 The Five Interpolation Schemes (Module 2 Syllabus)

The KTU 2024 scheme lists the following five canonical methods:

| # | Method | Best Use Case | Data Type |
|---|--------|---------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Newton's Forward Difference** | Interpolation **near the beginning** of equally spaced data | Equispaced |
| 2 | **Newton's Backward Difference** | Interpolation **near the end** of equally spaced data | Equispaced |
| 3 | **Newton's Divided Difference** | Interpolation for **unequally spaced** data | Non-equispaced |
| 4 | **Lagrange's Interpolation** | Direct polynomial fit for **unequally spaced** data | Non-equispaced |
| 5 | **Cubic Spline Interpolation** | Smooth, **piecewise** fitting for large data sets | Both |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Connect-the-Dots" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you have 5 lighthouses at known positions along a coastline, and you want to know where a ship is at an unknown point between two lighthouses. If you draw a smooth curve passing through all 5 lighthouse positions, then any point on that curve is your *interpolated* ship location.
>
> - **Newton's methods** are like drawing the curve using *forward-looking* or *backward-looking* pencils.
> - **Lagrange's method** builds the curve from the perspective of *each lighthouse individually*, then combines them.
> - **Cubic Spline** is like bending a *flexible ruler* (spline) through the lighthouses, ensuring the ruler doesn't have sharp kinks.

### 1.4 Key Terminology

- **Nodes (data points):** The given $(x_i, y_i)$ pairs.
- **Step size ($h$):** For equispaced data, $h = x_{i+1} - x_i$ (constant).
- **Degree of polynomial:** For $n+1$ data points, a polynomial of degree $\leq n$ is unique.
- **Forward Difference Operator ($\Delta$):** $\Delta y_i = y_{i+1} - y_i$.
- **Backward Difference Operator ($\nabla$):** $\nabla y_i = y_i - y_{i-1}$.
- **Divided Difference Operator:** $f[x_i, x_{i+1}] = \dfrac{f(x_{i+1}) - f(x_i)}{x_{i+1} - x_i}$.
- **Truncation Error:** The difference between the true function and the interpolating polynomial.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Forward Difference Interpolation

### 2.1.1 Theoretical Foundation

This formula uses **forward differences** built from the table. The interpolating polynomial is expressed as a sum of forward differences at the **first** point $x_0$.

Let $s = \dfrac{x - x_0}{h}$. Then:

$$\boxed{P_n(x) = \binom{s}{0} y_0 + \binom{s}{1} \Delta y_0 + \binom{s}{2} \Delta^2 y_0 + \binom{s}{3} \Delta^3 y_0 + \cdots + \binom{s}{n} \Delta^n y_0}$$

where the **generalized binomial** coefficient is:

$$\binom{s}{r} = \frac{s(s-1)(s-2)\cdots(s-r+1)}{r!}$$

### 2.1.2 Forward Difference Table Structure

| $x$ | $y$ | $\Delta y$ | $\Delta^2 y$ | $\Delta^3 y$ | $\cdots$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_0$ | $y_0$ | $\Delta y_0$ | $\Delta^2 y_0$ | $\Delta^3 y_0$ | $\cdots$ |
| $x_1$ | $y_1$ | $\Delta y_1$ | $\Delta^2 y_1$ | $\cdots$ | |
| $x_2$ | $y_2$ | $\Delta y_2$ | $\cdots$ | | |
| $x_3$ | $y_3$ | $\cdots$ | | | |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | | | | |

**Why use it?** Newton's Forward is best when $x$ is **close to $x_0$** (i.e., $0 \leq s \leq 1$). Common in KTU problems where the first row of a difference table is highlighted.

---

## 2.2 Newton's Backward Difference Interpolation

### 2.2.1 Theoretical Foundation

This formula uses **backward differences** at the **last** point $x_n$. Let $s = \dfrac{x - x_n}{h}$. Then:

$$\boxed{P_n(x) = \binom{s}{0} y_n + \binom{s+1}{1} \nabla y_n + \binom{s+2}{2} \nabla^2 y_n + \binom{s+3}{3} \nabla^3 y_n + \cdots + \binom{s+n}{n} \nabla^n y_n}$$

The general term is:

$$\binom{s+r-1}{r} = \frac{s(s+1)(s+2)\cdots(s+r-1)}{r!}$$

**Why use it?** When $x$ is **close to $x_n$** (i.e., $x$ is near the end of the table). The "look-back" structure makes it ideal for the last few entries.

---

## 2.3 Newton's Divided Difference Interpolation

### 2.3.1 Theoretical Foundation

Used when the $x$-values are **unequally spaced**. The interpolating polynomial is:

$$\boxed{P_n(x) = f[x_0] + (x - x_0)f[x_0, x_1] + (x - x_0)(x - x_1)f[x_0, x_1, x_2] + \cdots + \prod_{i=0}^{n-1}(x - x_i)\, f[x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n]}$$

**Recursive Divided Difference Definition:**

$$f[x_i, x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_{i+k}] = \frac{f[x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_{i+k}] - f[x_i, \ldots, x_{i+k-1}]}{x_{i+k} - x_i}$$

### 2.3.2 Divided Difference Table

| $x$ | $f[\cdot]$ | 1st DD | 2nd DD | 3rd DD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_0$ | $f[x_0]$ | $f[x_0, x_1]$ | $f[x_0, x_1, x_2]$ | $f[x_0, x_1, x_2, x_3]$ |
| $x_1$ | $f[x_1]$ | $f[x_1, x_2]$ | $f[x_1, x_2, x_3]$ | |
| $x_2$ | $f[x_2]$ | $f[x_2, x_3]$ | | |
| $x_3$ | $f[x_3]$ | | | |

**Why use it?** Most general Newton's form. Also forms the basis of **Hermite interpolation** and **Newton-Cotes quadrature** in higher modules.

---

## 2.4 Lagrange's Interpolation

### 2.4.1 Theoretical Foundation

Lagrange's formula directly constructs the polynomial as a **weighted sum of basis polynomials** $L_i(x)$:

$$\boxed{P_n(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} y_i \, L_i(x)}$$

where the **cardinal function** (Lagrange basis polynomial) is:

$$L_i(x) = \prod_{\substack{j=0 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j} = \frac{(x - x_0)(x - x_1)\cdots(x - x_{i-1})(x - x_{i+1})\cdots(x - x_n)}{(x_i - x_0)(x_i - x_1)\cdots(x_i - x_{i-1})(x_i - x_{i+1})\cdots(x_i - x_n)}$$

**Key Property:** $L_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$ (Kronecker delta) — equals 1 at $x_i$ and 0 at all other $x_j$.

**Why use it?** Elegant and symmetric in $x_i$. Best when only a **single interpolation** is required (no need to add more points later). Unlike Newton, it does not reuse prior computations.

---

## 2.5 Cubic Spline Interpolation

### 2.5.1 Theoretical Foundation

A **spline** is a piecewise polynomial. A **cubic spline** uses a **cubic polynomial on each subinterval** $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$, with smoothness conditions ensuring the pieces join smoothly.

For the $i$-th subinterval (where $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-1$):

$$\boxed{S_i(x) = a_i + b_i(x - x_i) + c_i(x - x_i)^2 + d_i(x - x_i)^3, \quad x \in [x_i, x_{i+1}]}$$

This gives **$4n$ unknowns** ($a_i, b_i, c_i, d_i$ for $i = 0, \ldots, n-1$).

### 2.5.2 The $4n$ Conditions

| # | Condition Type | Equation | Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Interpolation** at left node | $S_i(x_i) = y_i$ | $n$ |
| 2 | **Interpolation** at right node | $S_i(x_{i+1}) = y_{i+1}$ | $n$ |
| 3 | **Continuity of 1st derivative** | $S_i'(x_{i+1}) = S_{i+1}'(x_{i+1})$ | $n-1$ |
| 4 | **Continuity of 2nd derivative** | $S_i''(x_{i+1}) = S_{i+1}''(x_{i+1})$ | $n-1$ |
| 5 | **Boundary** (Natural spline) | $S_0''(x_0) = 0$ and $S_{n-1}''(x_n) = 0$ | $2$ |

**Total:** $n + n + (n-1) + (n-1) + 2 = 4n$ ✓

### 2.5.3 Tridiagonal System for Natural Cubic Spline

Defining $M_i = S''(x_i)$, the spline coefficients become:

$$d_i = \frac{M_{i+1} - M_i}{6 h_i}, \quad c_i = \frac{M_i}{2}, \quad b_i = \frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h_i} - \frac{h_i(2M_i + M_{i+1})}{6}, \quad a_i = y_i$$

where $h_i = x_{i+1} - x_i$. The $M_i$ satisfy the **tridiagonal system** ($M_0 = M_n = 0$):

$$\boxed{h_{i-1} M_{i-1} + 2(h_{i-1} + h_i) M_i + h_i M_{i+1} = 6\left[\frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h_i} - \frac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h_{i-1}}\right]}$$

for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$.

### 2.5.4 Other Spline Boundary Conditions

- **Natural (most common):** $M_0 = M_n = 0$ (zero curvature at endpoints).
- **Clamped (Not-a-Knot alternative):** $S_0'(x_0) = S_{n-1}'(x_n) = 0$ (zero slope at endpoints).
- **Not-a-Knot:** The 1st and 2nd pieces are combined at $x_1$, similarly at $x_{n-1}$.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Method | Formula | Best When |
|---|---|---|
| **Newton's Forward** | $P_n(x) = \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{s}{r} \Delta^r y_0$, $s = (x - x_0)/h$ | Equispaced, $x$ near start |
| **Newton's Backward** | $P_n(x) = \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{s+r-1}{r} \nabla^r y_n$, $s = (x - x_n)/h$ | Equispaced, $x$ near end |
| **Newton's Divided Diff.** | $P_n(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \left[\prod_{j=0}^{k-1}(x - x_j)\right] f[x_0, \ldots, x_k]$ | Unequally spaced |
| **Lagrange's** | $P_n(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} y_i \prod_{j \neq i} \dfrac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}$ | Unequally spaced, one-shot |
| **Cubic Spline** | $S_i(x) = a_i + b_i(x - x_i) + c_i(x - x_i)^2 + d_i(x - x_i)^3$ | Smooth piecewise, large $n$ |

### 2.7 Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> - **CAD/CAM:** Splines generate smooth curves in car body design, aircraft wings, and ship hulls.
> - **Signal Processing:** Spline interpolation for audio sample rate conversion.
> - **Computer Graphics:** Bézier curves (generalization of cubic splines) for font rendering.
> - **Geophysics:** Predicting ore body shapes from borehole data.
> - **Numerical PDEs:** Cubic splines are smoother than linear interpolation for finite element meshes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Newton's Forward Difference Formula

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: Taylor Series Analogy

Given a function $f(x)$ with step size $h$, the Taylor expansion about $x_0$ is:

$$f(x) = f(x_0) + (x - x_0) f'(x_0) + \frac{(x - x_0)^2}{2!} f''(x_0) + \frac{(x - x_0)^3}{3!} f'''(x_0) + \cdots$$

Now substitute $x = x_0 + s h$ so that $s = (x - x_0)/h$:

$$f(x_0 + sh) = f(x_0) + sh f'(x_0) + \frac{(sh)^2}{2!} f''(x_0) + \frac{(sh)^3}{3!} f'''(x_0) + \cdots$$

### 3.1.2 Replacing Derivatives with Forward Differences

Using the forward difference approximations:
- $f'(x_0) \approx \dfrac{\Delta y_0}{h}$
- $f''(x_0) \approx \dfrac{\Delta^2 y_0}{h^2}$
- $f'''(x_0) \approx \dfrac{\Delta^3 y_0}{h^3}$

Substituting:

$$f(x) = f(x_0) + sh \cdot \frac{\Delta y_0}{h} + \frac{(sh)^2}{2!} \cdot \frac{\Delta^2 y_0}{h^2} + \frac{(sh)^3}{3!} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3 y_0}{h^3} + \cdots$$

Simplifying powers of $h$:

$$f(x) = y_0 + s \Delta y_0 + \frac{s^2}{2!} \Delta^2 y_0 + \frac{s^3}{3!} \Delta^3 y_0 + \cdots$$

Reorganizing using generalized binomial coefficients:

$$\boxed{P_n(x) = \binom{s}{0} y_0 + \binom{s}{1} \Delta y_0 + \binom{s}{2} \Delta^2 y_0 + \binom{s}{3} \Delta^3 y_0 + \cdots + \binom{s}{n} \Delta^n y_0}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each derivative $f^{(k)}(x_0)$ is approximated by $\Delta^k y_0 / h^k$, and the factor $(x - x_0)^k / k!$ becomes $s^k h^k / k! = s(s-1)\cdots(s-k+1) \cdot h^k / k!$, which yields $\binom{s}{k} h^k \cdot \Delta^k y_0 / h^k = \binom{s}{k} \Delta^k y_0$. The $h^k$ terms **cancel** elegantly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Lagrange's Interpolation Formula

### 3.2.1 Constructing the Basis Polynomials

We want a polynomial $P_n(x)$ of degree $\leq n$ such that $P_n(x_i) = y_i$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$. Let:

$$P_n(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} y_i L_i(x)$$

where each $L_i(x)$ is a degree-$n$ polynomial satisfying:

$$L_i(x_j) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } j = i \\ 0 & \text{if } j \neq i \end{cases}$$

### 3.2.2 Building $L_i(x)$

Since $L_i(x)$ has $n$ roots at $x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_n$, it must be of the form:

$$L_i(x) = C_i \prod_{\substack{j=0 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} (x - x_j)$$

where $C_i$ is a constant. Imposing $L_i(x_i) = 1$:

$$1 = C_i \prod_{\substack{j=0 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} (x_i - x_j) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_i = \frac{1}{\prod_{j \neq i}(x_i - x_j)}$$

Therefore:

$$L_i(x) = \prod_{\substack{j=0 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}$$

**Conversion Logic:** A polynomial of degree $n$ has at most $n$ real roots. We force $L_i$ to vanish at $n$ specific points (all $x_j$ except $x_i$), and a single normalization constant makes the value 1 at $x_i$.

### 3.2.3 Final Formula

$$\boxed{P_n(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} y_i \prod_{\substack{j=0 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Lagrange's Interpolation (Numerical)

**Problem (KTU-typical):** Given $f(0) = 1$, $f(1) = 3$, $f(2) = 7$, find $f(1.5)$.

**Solution:**

For $n = 2$, the Lagrange polynomial is:

$$P_2(x) = y_0 L_0(x) + y_1 L_1(x) + y_2 L_2(x)$$

Compute each $L_i(x)$:

$$L_0(x) = \frac{(x - 1)(x - 2)}{(0 - 1)(0 - 2)} = \frac{(x - 1)(x - 2)}{2}$$

$$L_1(x) = \frac{(x - 0)(x - 2)}{(1 - 0)(1 - 2)} = \frac{x(x - 2)}{-1} = -x(x - 2)$$

$$L_2(x) = \frac{(x - 0)(x - 1)}{(2 - 0)(2 - 1)} = \frac{x(x - 1)}{2}$$

Now substitute $x = 1.5$:

$$L_0(1.5) = \frac{(0.5)(-0.5)}{2} = \frac{-0.25}{2} = -0.125$$

$$L_1(1.5) = -(1.5)(-0.5) = 0.75$$

$$L_2(1.5) = \frac{(1.5)(0.5)}{2} = \frac{0.75}{2} = 0.375$$

**Check:** $L_0 + L_1 + L_2 = -0.125 + 0.75 + 0.375 = 1$ ✓ (Lagrange basis sums to 1)

Therefore:

$$P_2(1.5) = (1)(-0.125) + (3)(0.75) + (7)(0.375) = -0.125 + 2.25 + 2.625 = 4.75$$

**Answer:** $f(1.5) = 4.75$.

**Conversion Logic at each step:** The numerators are formed by substituting $x = 1.5$ into $(x - x_j)$ for all $j \neq i$, and denominators are constants computed once at the start.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Natural Cubic Spline Equations

### 3.4.1 Continuity of 2nd Derivative

For $S_i(x) = a_i + b_i(x - x_i) + c_i(x - x_i)^2 + d_i(x - x_i)^3$:

$$S_i'(x) = b_i + 2c_i(x - x_i) + 3d_i(x - x_i)^2$$

$$S_i''(x) = 2c_i + 6d_i(x - x_i)$$

At $x = x_i$: $S_i''(x_i) = 2c_i = M_i$, hence $c_i = M_i / 2$.

At $x = x_{i+1}$: $S_i''(x_{i+1}) = 2c_i + 6d_i h_i = M_{i+1}$, hence $d_i = (M_{i+1} - M_i) / (6h_i)$.

### 3.4.2 Continuity of 1st Derivative

At $x = x_{i+1}$:

$$S_i'(x_{i+1}) = b_i + 2c_i h_i + 3d_i h_i^2 = b_i + M_i h_i + \frac{(M_{i+1} - M_i) h_i^2}{2h_i} = b_i + \frac{h_i(M_i + M_{i+1})}{2}$$

The next piece $S_{i+1}(x)$ has derivative at its left end ($x = x_{i+1}$):

$$S_{i+1}'(x_{i+1}) = b_{i+1} = b_{i+1}$$

Setting them equal:

$$b_i + \frac{h_i(M_i + M_{i+1})}{2} = b_{i+1}$$

This is the standard continuity equation used to derive the tridiagonal system.

### 3.4.3 Final Tridiagonal Form

The tridiagonal system for the $M_i$ (for natural spline, $M_0 = M_n = 0$) is:

$$h_{i-1} M_{i-1} + 2(h_{i-1} + h_i) M_i + h_i M_{i+1} = 6\left[\frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h_i} - \frac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h_{i-1}}\right]$$

for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$.

**Conversion Logic:** The left side comes from requiring the 1st derivative to be continuous across internal nodes; the right side is the difference in slopes $S_i'(x_{i+1}) - S_{i+1}'(x_{i+1})$ expressed purely in terms of $y$ and $h$ values.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Newton's Forward Difference (Numerical)

**Problem:** Given the data:

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 1 | 2 | 4 | 8 | 16 |

Estimate $y$ at $x = 1.5$.

**Forward Difference Table:**

| $x$ | $y$ | $\Delta y$ | $\Delta^2 y$ | $\Delta^3 y$ | $\Delta^4 y$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 0 | |
| 2 | 4 | 4 | 1 | | |
| 3 | 8 | 8 | | | |
| 4 | 16 | | | | |

Here $h = 1$, $x_0 = 0$. We need $s = (1.5 - 0)/1 = 1.5$.

$$P_4(1.5) = y_0 + s \Delta y_0 + \frac{s(s-1)}{2!} \Delta^2 y_0 + \frac{s(s-1)(s-2)}{3!} \Delta^3 y_0 + \frac{s(s-1)(s-2)(s-3)}{4!} \Delta^4 y_0$$

$$P_4(1.5) = 1 + (1.5)(1) + \frac{(1.5)(0.5)}{2}(1) + \frac{(1.5)(0.5)(-0.5)}{6}(0) + \cdots$$

$$P_4(1.5) = 1 + 1.5 + 0.375 + 0 = 2.875$$

(Note: the true function here appears to be $y = 2^x$, so $f(1.5) = 2^{1.5} \approx 2.828$, and our polynomial interpolates the data points but does not match the underlying function — this illustrates the distinction between *interpolation* and *function fitting*.)

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Right Interpolation Method

This diagram helps KTU students select the appropriate technique based on data spacing and position of $x$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Need to estimate y at given x]
    check1{Is data equally spaced?}
    check2{Where is x in the data table?}

    neq[Data is NON-EQUISPACED]
    eq[Data is EQUISPACED]

    lagrange[Lagrange Interpolation]
    divdiff[Newton Divided Difference]
    nf[Newtons Forward Difference]
    nb[Newtons Backward Difference]
    spl[Cubic Spline]

    q1{Large n and smoothness needed?}
    q2{x near start?}
    q3{x near end?}

    startA --> check1
    check1 -- Yes --> eq
    check1 -- No --> neq
    eq --> check2
    check2 -- Near start --> nf
    check2 -- Near end --> nb
    check2 -- Middle --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> spl
    q1 -- No --> nf
    neq --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> divdiff
    q2 -- No --> lagrange
    lagrange --> divdiff
    divdiff --> spl
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Cubic Spline Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Data x_i and y_i for i=0 to n]
    stepA[Compute step sizes h_i = x_i+1 - x_i]
    stepB[Compute slope differences d_i = y_i+1 - y_i / h_i]
    stepC[Build tridiagonal matrix A of size n-1 x n-1]
    stepD[Solve AM = b for second derivatives M_1 to M_n-1]
    stepE[Set boundary M_0 = 0 and M_n = 0]
    stepF[Compute coefficients a_i, b_i, c_i, d_i for each interval]
    stepG[Output spline S_i x on interval i]

    inputA --> stepA
    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepE --> stepD
    stepD --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Comparison of the Four Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Newton Forward]
    nodeB[Newton Backward]
    nodeC[Newton Divided Diff]
    nodeD[Lagrange]
    nodeE[Cubic Spline]

    nodeF[Single polynomial Forward s x x_0]
    nodeG[Single polynomial Backward s x x_n]
    nodeH[Single polynomial General spacing]
    nodeI[Single polynomial Symmetric in nodes]
    nodeJ[Piecewise cubic Smooth C2]

    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeJ

    nodeK[Add new point easily]
    nodeL[Best for single use]
    nodeM[Best for large smooth data]

    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeM
```
